channel.title: ETHICAL DILEMMAS and PERSPECTIVES in PSYCHOLOGICAL RESEARCH on Prof. Kirjakovski IBU Blog
channel.description: Recent content in ETHICAL DILEMMAS and PERSPECTIVES in PSYCHOLOGICAL RESEARCH on Prof. Kirjakovski IBU Blog

item.title: #05: Ethics in Psychotherapy
item.description:
Lecture Summary:

The ethical architecture of modern psychotherapy rests upon a delicate balance between foundational principles and the evolving complexities of clinical practice. At its core, the therapeutic relationship is sustained by four indispensable pillars: confidentiality, autonomy, beneficence, and non-maleficence. These concepts provide a structural framework that ensures patient welfare remains the primary focus, even as practitioners navigate competing professional paradigms that view therapy variously as a science, an art, or a form of social control. The transition from traditional paternalistic models toward a more collaborative, transparent partnership is facilitated by the use of formal therapeutic contracts and dynamic empowered consent. This shift acknowledges that while clinician expertise is vital, it must be integrated with patient preferences and validated empirical evidence to foster a truly effective healing environment.

item.title: #04: Diversity and Cultural Awareness
item.description:
Lecture Summary:

Modern clinical psychology is undergoing a necessary paradigm shift, moving away from traditional frameworks that have historically favored individualistic, Western-centric values. This evolution recognizes that culture is not merely a peripheral variable but a comprehensive foundational lifestyle that dictates how individuals perceive and evaluate reality. As demographic shifts continue to increase the diversity of patient populations, professionals are ethically mandated to move beyond singular dominant perspectives. Relying on standardized models that prioritize absolute personal autonomy and radical independence often fails to account for clients from communal backgrounds who value familial hierarchies and collective interdependence. Consequently, cultural competence is now understood as a foundational ethical floor of practice, essential for avoiding demonstrable clinical harm and ensuring that psychological interventions are both structurally sound and personally relevant.

item.title: #03: Privacy, Confidentiality, and Record Keeping
item.description:
Lecture Summary:

In the landscape of social science and psychological research, the concepts of privacy and confidentiality serve as the ethical bedrock for engaging with human participants. While privacy is defined as a state of limited access to an individual’s physical, informational, and decisional spheres, confidentiality represents the specific duty of a researcher to protect information disclosed in trust. These obligations are justified through both rights-based arguments, which emphasize the fundamental autonomy of the person, and consequentialist models, which suggest that without guaranteed secrecy, participants would be unwilling to provide the sensitive data necessary for the advancement of knowledge. Because researchers often initiate interactions that offer little direct benefit to the participant, they carry a profound ethical burden to ensure that no adverse effects arise from the collection, analysis, or archiving of personal records.

item.title: #02: Competence and Training
item.description:
Lecture Summary:

Professional competence in the mental health field is an evolving, lifelong commitment that extends far beyond the initial acquisition of an academic degree. It is rooted in the ethical principles of beneficence and non-maleficence, requiring practitioners to balance their clinical skills with a deep sense of self-awareness and personal wellness. Because competence is not a static achievement but a continuous process, professionals must rigorously monitor their own limitations and stay current with emerging research and shifting legal standards. When a client’s needs exceed a practitioner’s specialized training or the resources of a particular setting, the ethical course of action necessitates a skillful referral. Such transitions must be handled with transparency and care to ensure the client’s welfare remains the primary focus, preventing the potential harm that arises from clinical incompetence.

item.title: #01: Foundations of Ethical Practice
item.description:
Lecture Summary:

Ethical practice in psychological research did not emerge fully formed. It evolved through tension, crisis, and critical reflection. The contemporary landscape reflects a long-standing friction between regulatory systems shaped by biomedicine and the lived realities of social science research.

At the heart of this tension lies a mismatch. Biomedical ethics developed around a model focused on preventing physical harm, emphasizing protection and procedural control. Social science research, by contrast, often unfolds in fluid, relational, and context-sensitive environments. The resulting frustration—sometimes described as “ethics creep”—stems not from disagreement about whether ethics matter, but from disagreement about how ethics should be enacted in practice. Both regulators and researchers share the same premise: research must be morally justified. The challenge is moving from mere compliance toward intelligent, context-aware ethical conduct.

item.title: Course Reading Materials
item.description:
Reading Materials:

Mandatory:

  • Israel, M. (2015). Research ethics and integrity for social scientists: Beyond regulatory compliance (2nd ed.). Sage. (DOWNLOAD)

Supplementary:

  • Beauchamp, T. L., & Childress, J. F. (2013). Principles of biomedical ethics (7th ed.). Oxford University Press. (DOWNLOAD)
  • Corey, G., Corey, M. S., & Corey, C. (2019). Issues & ethics in the helping professions (10th ed.). Cengage. (DOWNLOAD)
  • Kitchener, K. S. (2000). Foundations of ethical practice, research, and teaching in psychology. Lawrence Erlbaum Associates. (DOWNLOAD)
